title: Slow Recoveries and Unemployment Traps: Monetary Policy in a Time of Hysteresis

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This is a Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (Number 831, November 2017, revised August 2018) by Acharya, Bengui, Dogra, and Wee analyzing how temporary economic shocks can permanently reduce productive capacity through hysteresis effects. The authors model how unemployed workers' skill losses create multiple steady-state unemployment rates, and how monetary policy constrained by the zero lower bound may be unable to escape resulting unemployment traps.
key_points: Paper models how temporary shocks permanently scar economy's productive capacity via unemployed workers' skill losses
When monetary policy is constrained by the zero bound, large shocks can push economy into a permanent unemployment trap from which monetary policy cannot escape ex post
Authors argue monetary policy must avoid unemployment traps ex ante since it cannot escape them ex post
Model quantitatively accounts for slow U.S. recovery following the Great Recession
Paper suggests lack of swift monetary accommodation helps explain European periphery stagnation
Published November 2017, revised August 2018 - approximately 7-8 years before current date
why_it_matters: This research provides a theoretical framework for understanding why aggressive monetary accommodation during severe downturns may be critical to prevent permanent economic damage. If hysteresis effects are real, central banks face asymmetric risks where inaction during crises has compounding long-term consequences.
macro_context: The paper's findings suggest that during severe recessions, the costs of insufficient monetary accommodation extend beyond short-term output losses to permanent reductions in labor market capacity. This supports more aggressive policy responses during ZLB-constrained periods.
